hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Hotels and Accommodation -- Checking In and Making Requests Like a Pro
lesson.menu_desc: Da chegada em Nova York ao balcao do hotel: como fazer pedidos educados e resolver problemas com indirect questions ('Could you tell me...', 'I was wondering if...') e o vocabulario de hospedagem -- lobby, key card, minibar, adjoining room -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- Hotels and Accommodation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 12 -- Hotels and Accommodation

listenings.text: Good evening, Ms. Ito, this is Marcus calling from the front desk. I'm just following up on the requests you made when you checked in, so everything is ready for you. First, the room. You asked whether we could move you to a higher, quieter floor, away from the street, and we were happy to do that. You are now in room fourteen-oh-two, on the fourteenth floor, facing the courtyard. Your new key card is waiting for you at the desk whenever you come down. Second, the air conditioning. You mentioned that the unit in your first room was faulty and would not cool down. An engineer has already checked the new room, so that is taken care of, but if anything feels wrong, please just let me know and someone will be sent up right away. Third, you asked earlier whether it would be possible to extend your stay by one night, until Sunday. I can confirm that the room is available, so your checkout has been moved to Sunday at noon, and everything will be added to one bill that you can settle when you leave. And finally, you wanted to know if we could set up an adjoining room for your colleague when she arrives on Thursday. We have one held next to yours. So, everything you asked for is arranged. If you need anything at all during your stay, please pick up the phone and dial zero. Enjoy your evening in New York, Ms. Ito.

Welcome back to The Careful Traveler, the show for people who want to feel at home anywhere in the world. Today, one small skill that changes every hotel stay: how to ask for what you want without sounding rude, and without freezing up. Here is the problem. In your own language, you know exactly how to be polite. But in English, under pressure, at a busy front desk, many people go too direct. They say, give me another room, or, where is the elevator, and it comes out sharp, even when they are lovely people. There is a simple fix, and once you have it, you will use it everywhere. It is called the indirect question. Instead of asking directly, you put a soft opener in front. Watch what happens. Where is the gym becomes could you tell me where the gym is. Is breakfast included becomes do you know if breakfast is included. Can I have a late checkout becomes I was wondering if it would be possible to have a late checkout. Notice two things. First, the opener does all the polite work: could you tell me, do you know, I was wondering if, would it be possible to. Second, and this is where everyone slips, after the opener the sentence goes back to normal order. It is where the gym is, not where is the gym. It is if breakfast is included, not is breakfast included. The question mark moves to the front, and the rest relaxes. Here is your challenge for your next trip. Every time you want to ask for something at the front desk, start with could you tell me or I was wondering if. A better room, a quieter floor, a later checkout, a fix for a faulty air conditioner. You are not asking for less. You are asking in a way that makes the person on the other side want to help you. And that, everywhere in the world, is the whole game.

paras: The flight had landed at JFK a little after six, and by the time the taxi reached the hotel, Lucimara wanted only one thing: her room. She crossed the bright lobby, rolled her suitcase to the front desk, and gave her name. The young man behind the counter typed for a moment and frowned. "I'm sorry," he said, "I don't see an Ito. I have a reservation under Yto, Y-T-O." A year ago, this was exactly the moment that would have frozen her. This time she stayed calm. "Could you tell me if that reservation is for two nights, arriving today?" she asked. He checked. It was. "Then I think that's mine," she said gently. "It's Ito, I-T-O. There's often a mix-up with the spelling." He smiled, corrected it, and the problem was gone.

"You're in room three-oh-four," he said, sliding a key card across the counter. "First floor, facing the street." Lucimara had stayed in enough hotels to know what a first-floor room facing the street in Manhattan sounded like at night. She did not complain, and she did not demand. She simply asked. "I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher floor, somewhere a little quieter. Do you know whether anything is available facing the back?" The clerk looked at his screen. There was a room on the fourteenth floor, facing the courtyard, at the same rate for double occupancy. "That would be perfect," she said. "Thank you."

In the new room, one thing was wrong. The air conditioning was faulty: it hummed loudly and blew warm air, and after ten minutes the room was no cooler. She picked up the phone and called the front desk, and again she led with a soft opener instead of an order. "I'm sorry to bother you," she said, "but the air conditioning doesn't seem to be working. Would you mind sending someone up to take a look? And could you tell me roughly how long it might take?" Twenty minutes, he said, and an engineer was at her door in fifteen. While she waited, she noticed the minibar, the courtyard view, the quiet. She thought about her colleague, arriving Thursday, and made a note to ask the desk whether they could set up an adjoining room.

On the last morning, Lucimara went down early. She had one more request, and by now it felt like nothing at all. "Good morning," she said. "I was wondering if I could extend my stay by one night, and whether I could settle the whole bill when I leave on Sunday." The clerk checked, confirmed the room was free, and moved everything onto a single bill. Two years ago, in a hotel exactly like this one, she would have pointed, smiled, and hoped someone understood. This week she had corrected a reservation, changed her room, reported a problem, and extended her stay, all in English, all politely, all by herself. The front desk on West 44th had no idea it had just watched a small, quiet victory.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: New York hotels are badly organized, and Lucimara had to fight with the front desk to get anything done.

You get what you need at a hotel not by demanding, but by asking politely -- a soft opener ('Could you tell me...', 'I was wondering if...') turns every request, even a complaint, into something the other person wants to help with.
Lucimara could not make herself understood in English, so she pointed and hoped, exactly as she had two years earlier.

items: The reservation was first found under the name "Yto" instead of "Ito".
"I have a reservation under Yto, Y-T-O." Lucimara sorted it out by asking an indirect question instead of freezing: "Could you tell me if that reservation is for two nights?"
Lucimara demanded a different room in an angry voice.
"She did not complain, and she did not demand. She simply asked: 'I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher floor.'" A soft opener, not a demand.
The first room she was given was on the first floor, facing the street.
"You're in room three-oh-four. First floor, facing the street." She knew what that sounds like at night in Manhattan, so she asked to move.
The air conditioning in the new room worked perfectly.
"The air conditioning was faulty: it hummed loudly and blew warm air." Faulty means it was not working correctly. She reported it politely.
On the last morning, Lucimara asked to extend her stay and settle the whole bill on Sunday.
"I was wondering if I could extend my stay by one night, and whether I could settle the whole bill when I leave on Sunday." To settle the bill means to pay the final total.

items.situation: You reach the front desk and your name is not in the system. Ask -- politely and indirectly -- whether the reservation might be under a different spelling.
tips: Could you tell me if the reservation might be under a different spelling? It's Ito, I-T-O -- there's often a mix-up.
Start with 'Could you tell me if...'. After the opener, normal order: 'if the reservation might be...', never 'if might be the reservation'.

items.situation: Your room faces a noisy street. Ask if you can move to a higher, quieter floor.
tips: I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher floor, somewhere a little quieter.
'I was wondering if it would be possible to...' is the softest, most natural way to ask for an upgrade or a change. It never sounds like a demand.

items.situation: You want to know whether breakfast is included in the rate. Ask indirectly.
tips: Do you know if breakfast is included in the rate?
Direct: 'Is breakfast included?' Indirect: 'Do you know IF breakfast IS included?' -- the 'if' comes in and the verb goes to the end, in normal order.

items.situation: The air conditioning is faulty. Report it politely and ask how long a repair might take.
tips: I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning seems to be faulty. Would you mind sending someone up, and could you tell me how long it might take?
'Would you mind sending...' + 'could you tell me how long it might take' -- two soft openers. Notice: 'how long it might take', not 'how long might it take'.

items.situation: A colleague arrives Thursday. Ask whether they can set up an adjoining room next to yours.
tips: I was wondering whether it would be possible to have an adjoining room for my colleague, arriving on Thursday.
'whether' works just like 'if' after the opener. An adjoining room is one connected to yours by an inside door.

items.situation: It's your last morning. Ask to extend your stay by a night and to settle the whole bill on checkout.
tips: Could you tell me if I could extend my stay by one night, and settle the whole bill when I leave?
To 'extend your stay' is to stay longer; to 'settle the bill' is to pay the final total. One soft opener carries both requests.
